raw_premise: Design a study to understand how users interpret probabilistic/ambiguous outputs in decision-support tools, using divination-app affordances as a test case.
sharpest_question: What specific interpretive failure mode does a divination-app interface produce that a standard confidence-interval display does not — and why would that difference be theoretically meaningful rather than merely exotic?
claim: If this worked, it would establish that a specific affordance pattern drawn from divination UX (e.g., vague symbolic language, ritual framing, user-projection prompts) systematically shifts how users calibrate uncertainty in decision-support contexts — in a direction that existing ambiguity-display research has not characterized.
differentia: The differentia is not visible in the premise. 'Divination-app affordances as a test case' is a vehicle choice, not a research contribution. The premise does not say what property of divination UI is doing theoretical work, nor whether the claim is about calibration accuracy, decision confidence, over-reliance, or something else.
nearest_template: Ambiguity aversion and uncertainty communication in decision-support UI (Lipkus & Peters on numeric risk comprehension; Hullman et al. on uncertainty visualization; Cai et al. on AI confidence displays). The specific sub-pattern is: manipulate display format of probabilistic output → measure user interpretation accuracy or reliance behavior → compare conditions. This is a well-worn two-by-two.
missing_evidence: What decision is under study? The premise names a domain (decision-support tools) but not a choice being made — without this, you cannot operationalize 'interpretation.'

What is the specific affordance from divination apps being isolated? Symbolic ambiguity? Ritual interaction sequence? Personalization language? Without isolating one mechanism, the divination framing is decoration.
Who is the population? Divination apps attract specific demographics; if the study depends on prior divination familiarity, that is a confound not yet acknowledged.
What outcome variable distinguishes 'interprets well' from 'interprets poorly'? Calibration? Decision quality? Expressed uncertainty? Over-reliance on the tool?
What is the niche in existing uncertainty-display literature? The premise does not cite a gap — it implies one by novelty of context, which is not the same thing.
sharpened_options: Territory: Users systematically over-rely on AI decision-support outputs when displays project false precision. Niche: Existing work tests numeric vs. visual uncertainty formats but has not tested whether ambiguity-preserving language (the operational logic of horoscope-style framing) reduces over-reliance compared to probability displays. Occupation: Users shown horoscope-style ambiguous output for an AI hiring recommendation will show lower over-reliance (measured by deviation from tool output when given contradicting evidence) than users shown a percentage-confidence display, because ambiguous language forces active interpretation rather than anchoring.

Territory: Uncertainty displays in AI tools presuppose users have calibrated probabilistic intuitions. Niche: Prior work has not examined whether interactive ritual framing — the sequence of input, reflection prompt, and symbolic output common in divination apps — produces better subjective calibration than passive probability display by forcing users to articulate their prior before seeing the output. Occupation: A within-subjects study comparing a ritual-sequence uncertainty display to a standard confidence bar will show higher post-decision calibration accuracy and lower surprise ratings when outcomes are revealed, because the ritual sequence externalizes the user's prior expectation.

Territory: AI decision-support tools are adopted or rejected partly on the basis of perceived legitimacy of their uncertainty communication. Niche: Existing adoption studies compare expert vs. lay numeracy but have not tested whether symbolic/vague output framing increases perceived legitimacy among users with low numeracy — the population most vulnerable to miscalibration. Occupation: Low-numeracy users rate a divination-styled uncertainty display as more trustworthy and make decisions more aligned with their stated values than when shown a numeric probability, revealing a trust-calibration tradeoff that standard displays obscure.

Territory: Over-reliance on AI recommendations is a known failure mode, but interface-level mitigations remain underspecified. Niche: Prompting users to self-generate meaning before receiving a recommendation (a structural feature of divination UI) has not been tested as an over-reliance intervention against standard displays. Occupation: Users who generate a personal interpretation prompt before seeing an AI recommendation defer to the AI recommendation less often in adversarial cases — establishing projection-first sequencing as a candidate over-reliance mitigation independent of the divination aesthetic.
